title: How the Glorious Revolution crushed the NIMBYs
gist: The Industrial Revolution was enabled by a prior political revolution: after 1688, an empowered landowner Parliament dismantled overly rigid, fragmented property rights — through enclosure, breaking entails, and turnpike/canal infrastructure acts — by working with incumbents and compensating losers rather than overriding them. The piece argues today's Western 'vetocracy' is the same disease, and that supermajority, beneficiary-aligned reform (in housing and labor) is the proven cure.

themes.theme: Strong property rights as the obstacle, not weak ones

insights.claim: Early modern European growth stalled because property rights were too strong and too rigid, not too weak — fragmented ownership and inheritance rules meant no one investing in improvements could expect a return.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Reframes the institutional-economics consensus: the binding constraint on development can be overly entrenched rights, a lens applicable to today's zoning and regulatory gridlock.
insights.quote: Early modern Europe’s property rights held back growth not because they were too weak but because they were too strong and too rigid.

insights.claim: Continental reform efforts repeatedly failed because monarchs depended on landowning aristocrats and clergy who had no confidence reforms would leave them better off — Spain's Fernando VI (1746), France's Turgot (1776), and Austria's Joseph II (1781) were all blocked or nullified.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that top-down reform against incumbent stakeholders systematically fails — a template for why modern anti-NIMBY mandates stall.
insights.quote: monarchs depended on the support of landowning aristocrats and clergy, and those landowners had no confidence that reforms would leave them better off.
insights.locator: Many European states tried to solve

insights.claim: England uniquely modernized property rights because the Glorious Revolution handed the country to the landowners, who — precisely because they were empowered — dismantled the fossilized arrangements their continental counterparts could not touch.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Core counterintuitive mechanism: empowering incumbents, not overriding them, unlocked reform — the central transferable lesson of the piece.
insights.quote: The Glorious Revolution resolved the conflict between landowners and the Crown by handing the country to the landowners.

insights.claim: After the Glorious Revolution, requiring annual military-funding renewals forced Parliament to sit annually for over 100 days, raising output from about 25 acts per session (1660-1689) to 50-75 by 1688-1724 and eventually over 200 per session by the late eighteenth century.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: State capacity to legislate frequently and predictably was a precondition for reform throughput — implies modern reform requires institutional bandwidth, not just political will.
insights.quote: From 1660 to 1689, about 25 acts passed per session. Between 1688 and 1724, the average increased to 50–75 acts per session, rising to over 100 and eventually 200

insights.claim: Access to Parliament broadened after 1688: whereas only the largest landowners previously brought bills, many minor gentry with no political connections began applying, with the vast majority of bills seeking to reform or consolidate landholdings and property rights.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Broad-based legislative access channeled bottom-up demand for property reform — a model for distributing reform initiative beyond elite gatekeepers.
insights.quote: Before the Revolution, only the largest landowners brought bills to Parliament. Afterwards, many more minor gentry with no obvious political connections applied.

insights.claim: The open-field system and common land crippled productivity — under Russia's equivalent peasants wasted up to half their time moving between strips, and the Norfolk four-course rotation was nearly impossible on shared fields because turnips and clover could not be protected from neighbors' grazing animals.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Concretely quantifies the cost of fragmented rights, the historical analogue to land-assembly problems in modern development.
insights.quote: Under Russia’s equivalent system, peasants wasted as much as half their time moving between land strips.
insights.locator: The problems with small fields grew

insights.claim: Parliament progressively lowered the consent threshold for enclosure — from unanimity to 90 percent of affected landowners by the 1740s, 80 percent by the 1750s, and 75 percent later in the century — passing 3,682 enclosure acts between 1600 and 1815, almost all after 1688.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: The supermajority mechanism is the precise, replicable tool the author advocates for modern land assembly and densification.
insights.quote: By the 1740s, it was passing enclosure laws with 90 percent support of the landowners affected; by the 1750s, 80 percent; and later in the century, 75 percent.
insights.locator: Over the century following the Glorious Revolution

insights.claim: Supermajority field-consolidation schemes worked far beyond England: Germany consolidated land only after WWII (tripling wheat yields), Taiwan, Korea and Japan used similar schemes, Stolypin's reforms made Russia the world's top grain exporter, and consolidated 'rational squares' made the Punjab the breadbasket of India and Pakistan.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Establishes the supermajority-consolidation playbook as a repeatedly validated growth lever across very different polities.
insights.quote: Pyotr Stolypin’s land reforms used supermajority petitions by peasant farmers to divide their landholdings, boosting productivity so much that the country became the world’s top grain exporter.

insights.claim: Strict settlements and entails locked up estates so rigidly that owners could not sell, mortgage, or improve land without an heir's consent even before an heir was born — one case left timber uncut for 67 years until 'all the old trees... had become decayed and valueless.'
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Vivid illustration of value destruction from over-rigid rights, the historical mirror of modern preservation rules freezing assets.
insights.quote: for 67 years, ‘not a stick [had] been cut’ and ‘all the old trees, with scarcely an exception, [had] become decayed and valueless’.

insights.claim: Parliament broke settlements only when the living owner proposed improvements expected to benefit heirs too, using expropriation-with-compensation, passing 3,233 single-estate acts between 1689 and 1830 clustered around London and the industrializing North West.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Beneficiary-aligned, compensated reform faced little opposition — the design principle the author urges modern reformers to copy.
insights.quote: Parliament to pass 3,233 estate acts between 1689 and 1830, each tackling a single estate.
insights.locator: Parliament, now sitting far more often

insights.claim: Napoleon's 1804 Code Civil restricted entails top-down but caused morcellement — the excessive subdivision entails were meant to prevent — whereas England's consent-based approach avoided that problem and reformed faster than Spain (entails abolished 1841), Italy (1865), or Germany (1919).
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Top-down rights abolition produced a different failure mode; the lesson is that reform method, not just direction, determines outcomes.
insights.quote: the top-down approach caused a different problem across the territories where it was imposed: morcellement, the excessive subdivision that entails had been designed to prevent
insights.locator: It was possible to fix settlements

insights.claim: Fragmented tolling gave operators perverse incentives: in the Holy Roman Empire each road stretch overcharged without investing, and Rhine timber merchants between Andernach and Dordrecht paid around twenty separate tolls.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Splitting infrastructure into the wrong units destroys investment incentives — directly analogous to fragmented modern permitting and jurisdictional veto points.
insights.quote: they had to pay around twenty separate tolls.

insights.claim: Turnpike trusts — non-profit corporations of landowners and merchants who improved roads and charged cost-covering tolls — gave Britain 20,000 miles of turnpikes by 1840, over twice France's paved-road density and nearly ten times Spain's, quadrupling British travel speeds to eight mph by 1820.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Aligning infrastructure ownership with beneficiaries (who gained via higher land rents) solved the incentive problem — a model for value-capture infrastructure finance.
insights.quote: By 1840, Britain had 20,000 miles of turnpikes, more than twice France’s density of paved road per square mile, and nearly ten times Spain’s.

insights.claim: Combined road, river, and canal improvements cut transport costs by 75 percent, enabling regional specialization (Cheshire cheese, Stoke pottery, Sheffield steel) and a national wheat market, with the Bridgewater canal halving Manchester's coal costs.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Transport-cost collapse, not just invention, enabled industrial agglomeration — underscoring infrastructure as the multiplier on technology.
insights.quote: Together, improvements in roads, rivers, and canals cut transport costs by 75 percent.
insights.locator: Together, improvements in roads, rivers, and canals

insights.claim: The author argues today's zoning, historic preservation, judicial review, environmental regulation, and European dismissal-compensation rights are the modern analogue of premodern over-rigid property rights, producing a 'vetocracy' that paralyzes developed countries.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Maps a historical diagnosis onto present growth bottlenecks — a thesis for why housing supply and labor-market reform are stuck and how to unstick them.
insights.quote: Zoning, historic preservation, judicial review, environmental regulation all serve, among other things, to protect the property values of existing homeowners in the area.

insights.claim: The author claims many incumbents would gain from well-designed reform: in Palo Alto every homeowner could be paid tens of millions to accept Manhattan-level density while developers still clear hundreds of billions in profit.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies the surplus available to buy out NIMBY opposition — a concrete argument for compensation-based upzoning that could reshape housing supply economics.
insights.quote: In Palo Alto, every homeowner could be paid tens of millions of dollars to accept Manhattan-level density, and developers might still clear hundreds of billions in profit.
insights.locator: As in premodern Europe, the costs

insights.claim: The author points to Denmark's flexicurity — up to 90 percent of income for two years of unemployment insurance in exchange for giving up labor protections — as a modern beneficiary-aligned trade financed by higher growth.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A live example that compensating losers can unlock labor-market flexibility; relevant to European competitiveness and restructuring debates.
insights.quote: Danish workers receive extremely generous state unemployment insurance, up to 90 percent of income for two years, in exchange for giving up labor protections
insights.locator: As in premodern Europe, the costs

insights.claim: The author cites modern proof the method still works: Japan, Taiwan, and Korea used landowner supermajorities to replan roughly a third of their cities at higher density, Israel's Pinui Binui delivers many new homes, and UK social tenants have approved dozens of demolish-and-upgrade proposals.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Contemporary precedents make the supermajority/beneficiary-consent housing playbook actionable now, not merely historical.
insights.quote: Japan, Taiwan, and Korea each used supermajorities of landowners to replan roughly a third of their cities at higher densities with better infrastructure.
